title: Region, AZ, or Edge?
description:
## Placing the Tiers Correctly

A media company serves a single-page application backed by a large catalog of **static assets** (images, video thumbnails, JS/CSS) to a **global** audience that complains about slow page loads from distant geographies. The same application uses a **relational database** that must survive the failure of an individual data center within its primary Region without data loss, but does **not** need to be globally distributed.

The team must choose where each tier lives. Which design correctly maps each requirement to the appropriate layer of AWS global infrastructure?

options.text: Serve the static assets from an S3 bucket fronted by **CloudFront** (edge locations cache content close to users worldwide), and run the database on **Amazon RDS with Multi-AZ** in the primary Region.
Replicate the static assets into a bucket in every Availability Zone of the primary Region, and deploy the database to a CloudFront edge location for the lowest possible latency.
Deploy the entire application, including the database, to a single Availability Zone and enable Route 53 latency-based routing to reduce global latency.
Put the static assets in one S3 bucket in the primary Region with no CDN, and run the database across multiple Regions using cross-Region read replicas for AZ-level resilience.

explanation: **CloudFront** uses hundreds of **edge locations (PoPs)** to cache static content physically close to users, which is exactly what fixes global latency for static assets — origin S3 stays in one Region. For the database, **RDS Multi-AZ** maintains a synchronous standby in a *different Availability Zone* in the same Region and fails over automatically, satisfying "survive a single data center failure without data loss" — which is an AZ-level concern, not a Region-level one. Option B confuses concepts: you cannot run a database "at an edge location," and AZ-level bucket replication does nothing for global latency. Option C uses one AZ (no resilience at all) and latency-based routing across Regions you don't have. Option D over-engineers with cross-Region replicas (a multi-Region, not multi-AZ, construct), leaves global users without a CDN, and read replicas are asynchronous — not a substitute for Multi-AZ HA.

hints.title: Match the layer to the need
hints.body: Global low-latency content delivery is an edge/CloudFront problem. Surviving one data center in a Region is an Availability Zone problem. They are different layers.

hints.title: What 'Multi-AZ' actually means
hints.body: RDS Multi-AZ keeps a synchronous standby in another AZ within the same Region. It is for high availability inside a Region, not for global distribution.
